column1: ls -l shows -rwsr-xr-x on /usr/bin/passwd. What does the s mean and why is it needed?
column2: The s in the owner execute position is the setuid bit. When any user runs passwd, the process temporarily runs with root's permissions (passwd is owned by root). This allows writing to /etc/shadow which only root can access. Without setuid, users could never change their own passwords.

column1: You create a file in /var/www/project/. It shows your username as group instead of webteam. How do you fix this permanently for all future files?
column2: Set the setgid bit on the directory: chmod g+s /var/www/project/ or chmod 2775 /var/www/project/. With setgid, all new files created inside inherit the directory's group (webteam) instead of the creator's primary group. Existing files are unaffected — only new files follow the rule.

column1: What is umask 027? What permissions does it give to new files and directories?
column2: umask 027 removes write from group and all permissions from others. New files: 640 (rw-r-----). New dirs: 750 (rwx r-x ---). Production use: service accounts should use umask 027 so they cannot accidentally create world-readable files. Set in systemd unit with UMask=0027.

column1: A CI/CD pipeline gets Permission denied on deploy.sh. ls -l shows -rw-r--r--. What is wrong and what is the minimum fix?
column2: No execute bit. The script has 644 — read/write for owner, read for group and others, no execute for anyone. Shell cannot execute a script without the execute bit. Fix: chmod 755 deploy.sh (or chmod +x deploy.sh). The x bit is required for any user to run the script as a program.

column1: What does the + at end of permissions mean in ls -l? Example: -rw-r--r--+
column2: The + indicates an ACL (Access Control List) is set on this file beyond standard permissions. Standard ls -l cannot show the full picture. Use getfacl filename to see all ACL entries including per-user and per-group permissions.

column1: You need to give user ci-runner read access to /etc/nginx/nginx.conf without making it world-readable and without adding ci-runner to the root group. What do you use?
column2: ACL: setfacl -m u:ci-runner:r /etc/nginx/nginx.conf. Then verify: getfacl /etc/nginx/nginx.conf. This grants exactly read permission to one specific user without changing standard permissions, group membership, or world-readability. This is the primary use case for ACLs over standard permissions.

It applies execute permission to ALL files including regular HTML, CSS, JS files — not just directories. Regular files do not need the execute bit. The correct approach: find /var/www/html -type d -exec chmod 755 {} \\; && find /var/www/html -type f -exec chmod 644 {} \\;

column1: A file has permissions rw-r--r-- and you run chmod o=. What are the new permissions?
column2: rw-r----- (640). chmod o= sets other permissions to nothing (empty). Owner permissions (rw-) unchanged. Group permissions (r--) unchanged. Other permissions cleared to ---. Equivalent to: chmod 640 filename
